home.seoTitle: Free Online Tools for Developers and Website Operators
home.seoDescription: A collection of browser-based tools for formatting data, converting timestamps, encoding text, parsing User-Agent strings, and generating log analysis SQL.
home.h1: Free Online Tools for Developers and Website Operators
home.intro: A collection of browser-based tools for developers, data analysts, and website operators. These tools help you format data, convert timestamps, encode text, parse User-Agent strings, and generate log analysis SQL.
home.privacy: Most tools run locally in your browser. Your input is not uploaded unless explicitly stated, which makes the site useful for quick debugging without creating an account or sending private snippets to a remote service.
home.recommendedIntro: Start with the tools most commonly used for data cleanup, debugging, encoding, and log analysis.
home.staticTitle: Built for static pages and readable content
home.staticIntro: Each tool page includes a clear explanation, usage steps, examples, frequently asked questions, privacy notes, and links to related tools. The core page content is rendered as HTML during static generation, so visitors and search crawlers can read the page even when JavaScript is disabled.

toolsIndex.seoDescription: Browse free online tools for JSON formatting, timestamp conversion, URL encoding, Base64 conversion, User-Agent parsing, and Aliyun SLS SQL generation.
toolsIndex.intro: Browse free browser-based utilities for formatting data, converting dates, encoding text, checking User-Agent strings, and preparing Aliyun SLS SQL templates. Every tool is available without login and includes static documentation for quick reference.

about.seoDescription: Learn what Free Online Tools provides, who it is for, and how browser-based processing protects tool input by default.
about.intro: Free Online Tools is a small collection of practical utilities for developers, data analysts, website operators, and technical support teams. The first version focuses on common daily tasks: formatting JSON, converting Unix timestamps, encoding URL text, decoding Base64, parsing User-Agent strings, and generating Aliyun SLS SQL templates.

sections.title: What this site provides
sections.body: The site is designed around focused tools with readable explanations. Each tool page includes usage steps, an example, frequently asked questions, a privacy note, and links to related tools. The goal is to make the page useful both when you need a quick conversion and when you need to understand what the conversion means.

sections.title: Are the tools free?
sections.body: Yes. The tools in this first version are free to access and do not require registration. The site may later use third-party advertising or analytics to support maintenance, but the tools will remain accessible without login.

sections.title: Is my data uploaded?
sections.body: Tool input is processed locally in your browser by default. The static pages and client-side tool components are designed so that common formatting, encoding, decoding, and parsing tasks do not require sending your input to a server.

sections.title: Who is it for?
sections.body: The tools are useful for front-end engineers, back-end engineers, data analysts, QA testers, site operators, and anyone who needs quick browser-based utilities while debugging websites, logs, APIs, redirects, tracking events, or text payloads.

about.contactBody: For feedback, bug reports, and feature requests, contact

contact.seoDescription: Contact Free Online Tools for feedback, bug reports, and feature requests.
contact.intro: You can contact the site maintainer by email for feedback, bug reports, tool suggestions, or privacy questions.

sections.title: Bug reports
sections.body: When reporting a bug, include the tool name, the browser you used, the type of input that caused the issue, and the result you expected. Do not send private credentials, production secrets, or sensitive customer data.

sections.title: Feature requests
sections.body: Feature requests are welcome when they fit the site's purpose: fast browser-based utilities with clear documentation. Useful details include the problem you are trying to solve, sample input and output, and whether the tool should work fully offline in the browser.

sections.title: Privacy questions
sections.body: If you have questions about local processing, cookies, advertising services, or analytics, include the page URL and a short description of the concern so it can be reviewed clearly.

privacy.seoDescription: Read the Privacy Policy for Free Online Tools, including local tool processing, cookies, Google Analytics, future Google AdSense use, external links, and contact details.
privacy.intro: This Privacy Policy explains how Free Online Tools handles tool input, cookies, third-party services, Google Analytics, future advertising services such as Google AdSense, external links, and contact requests.

sections.title: Tool input data
sections.body: The tools in this first version process input locally in your browser by default. JSON formatting, timestamp conversion, URL encoding, Base64 conversion, User-Agent parsing, and SQL template generation do not require uploading your input to our server.

sections.title: Cookies
sections.body: The site may use cookies or similar browser storage for basic preferences, analytics, or future advertising services. You can manage or block cookies through your browser settings.

sections.title: Google Analytics
sections.body: The site may use Google Analytics to understand page performance, popular tools, and errors. Analytics is used for aggregate site operation insights and should not require users to submit private tool input.

sections.title: Future Google AdSense and advertising
sections.body: The site may use Google AdSense or other third-party advertising services in the future. These services may use cookies or similar technologies to show, measure, and improve ads according to their own policies.

sections.title: External links
sections.body: Some pages may link to external documentation or services. External sites have their own privacy policies, and this site is not responsible for their content or practices.

terms.seoDescription: Read the terms for using Free Online Tools, including acceptable use, disclaimers, and user responsibility.
terms.intro: By using Free Online Tools, you agree to use the site responsibly and understand that the tools are provided for convenience, debugging, learning, and reference.

sections.title: Tools are for reference
sections.body: The tools are intended to help with common technical tasks, but generated output is not guaranteed to be correct for every environment. Review results before using them in production systems, security-sensitive workflows, or business decisions.

sections.title: User responsibility
sections.body: You are responsible for the input you paste into the tools and for how you use the output. Do not paste passwords, access tokens, private keys, customer records, or other sensitive information unless you have verified that doing so is appropriate for your situation.

sections.title: Acceptable use
sections.body: Do not misuse the site, attempt to disrupt service availability, scrape pages aggressively, bypass normal access controls, or use the tools for unlawful activity.

sections.title: Disclaimer
sections.body: The site is provided as is, without warranties of accuracy, availability, or fitness for a particular purpose. Use the tools at your own risk.

jsonFormatter.seoDescription: Format, validate, and minify JSON data directly in your browser.
jsonFormatter.intro: Format, validate, and minify JSON data directly in your browser. This tool is useful when you need to inspect API responses, clean configuration files, check copied payloads, or reduce JSON into a compact single-line string.
howToUse: Paste a JSON object, array, string, number, boolean, or null value into the input box. Select Format to convert valid JSON into an indented structure, Minify to remove extra whitespace, or Validate to check whether the input can be parsed as JSON.

If the input contains a syntax problem, the tool shows the parsing error instead of changing the output. Common issues include missing commas, trailing commas, single quotes around strings, unescaped line breaks, and comments copied from JavaScript files.
jsonFormatter.example: A compact JSON value with fields like name and enabled can be formatted into a readable multi-line block. The same input can also be minified again for use in a request body, environment variable, or log field.
jsonFormatter.privacy: Your input is processed locally in your browser and is not uploaded to our server.

faq.a: JSON formatting adds indentation and line breaks to valid JSON so nested objects and arrays are easier to read.

faq.q: What is the difference between a formatter and a minifier?
faq.a: A formatter improves readability, while a minifier removes unnecessary whitespace to make the JSON shorter.

faq.q: Why does valid JavaScript fail as JSON?
faq.a: JSON is stricter than JavaScript object syntax. JSON requires double-quoted property names and does not allow comments or trailing commas.
No. The formatting, validation, and minifying logic runs locally in your browser.

timestampConverter.seoDescription: Convert Unix timestamps to local and UTC dates, and convert dates back to seconds and milliseconds timestamps.
timestampConverter.intro: Convert Unix timestamps into readable local and UTC dates, or convert a date and time back into seconds and milliseconds. The converter detects 10-digit second timestamps and 13-digit millisecond timestamps.
howToUse: To convert a timestamp to a date, paste a 10-digit Unix timestamp in seconds or a 13-digit timestamp in milliseconds, then run the conversion. The result shows the detected unit, local time, UTC time, seconds, and milliseconds.

To convert a date to a timestamp, choose a date and time in the date input. The result shows both seconds and milliseconds so you can use the value in APIs, logs, database fields, or analytics systems.
timestampConverter.example: The timestamp 1704067200 represents a seconds-based Unix timestamp. The same instant in milliseconds is 1704067200000. Many APIs use seconds, while JavaScript dates and browser timers usually use milliseconds.
timestampConverter.privacy: Your input is processed locally in your browser and is not uploaded to our server.

faq.a: A Unix timestamp counts time from 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC. It is often stored as seconds or milliseconds.

faq.q: How do I know if a timestamp is seconds or milliseconds?
faq.a: A 10-digit value is commonly seconds. A 13-digit value is commonly milliseconds.

faq.q: Why are local time and UTC different?
faq.a: UTC is a global reference time. Local time applies your device timezone offset, which can change the displayed hour or date.

urlEncoder.seoDescription: Encode and decode URL components for query strings, redirect URLs, tracking parameters, and web debugging.
urlEncoder.intro: Encode and decode URL components for query strings, redirect URLs, analytics parameters, and debugging. The tool uses browser URL component encoding so spaces, symbols, and non-ASCII text can be safely placed in a URL parameter.
howToUse: Paste plain text or a URL component into the input box and choose Encode to escape characters that have special meaning in URLs. Paste an encoded value and choose Decode to convert percent-encoded sequences back to readable text.

Decode errors usually happen when the input contains incomplete percent escapes such as %E0%A4 or a standalone percent sign. In that case, check the original value before using it in a redirect or request.
urlEncoder.example: The text hello world becomes hello%20world when encoded as a URL component. A full redirect URL can also be encoded before placing it inside another query parameter.
urlEncoder.privacy: Your input is processed locally in your browser and is not uploaded to our server.

faq.a: URL encoding converts characters into a safe percent-encoded form so they can be used inside URLs.

faq.q: When should I encode a URL value?
faq.a: Encode values before placing them in query parameters, redirect parameters, or tracking links where spaces and symbols could break parsing.

faq.q: What is the difference between encodeURI and encodeURIComponent?
faq.a: encodeURI keeps URL separators such as ? and &. encodeURIComponent encodes a single component more aggressively.

base64.seoDescription: Encode UTF-8 text to Base64 and decode Base64 strings back to readable text in your browser.
base64.intro: Encode UTF-8 text to Base64 and decode Base64 strings back to text. This tool is useful for inspecting API payloads, small configuration values, email content, and text snippets that have been represented in Base64 form.
howToUse: Paste readable text and choose Encode to convert it into Base64. Paste a Base64 string and choose Decode to recover the original UTF-8 text. Invalid Base64 input is caught and shown as an error instead of breaking the page.

The tool supports UTF-8 text, so non-English characters can be encoded and decoded for everyday debugging tasks. For binary files, use a dedicated file encoder instead of pasting large binary content into a text area.
base64.example: The text Hello becomes SGVsbG8= in Base64. The value is not encrypted; it is simply represented with a character set that is convenient for transport in text-based systems.
base64.privacy: Your input is processed locally in your browser and is not uploaded to our server.

faq.a: Base64 is an encoding method that represents data using letters, numbers, plus, slash, and equals characters.
No. Base64 does not hide or protect data. Anyone can decode it back to the original content.
Common uses include small API payloads, email formats, data URLs, tokens that are not secrets, and text-safe transport.

userAgentParser.seoDescription: Parse browser, operating system, device type, and rendering engine from a User-Agent string.
userAgentParser.intro: Parse a User-Agent string to identify the likely browser, operating system, device type, and rendering engine. This first version uses lightweight browser-side rules for quick diagnostics.
howToUse: Paste a browser User-Agent string into the input box and select Parse. The result shows a JSON object with browser, operating system, device type, and rendering engine fields.

User-Agent strings are not a perfect source of truth. Browsers may reduce, freeze, or spoof parts of the string, and some embedded webviews use custom formats. Treat the parsed result as a debugging hint rather than a security decision.
userAgentParser.example: A Chrome desktop User-Agent often contains Windows NT, AppleWebKit, Chrome, and Safari. The parser uses these markers to infer Windows, Chrome, Desktop, and Blink.
userAgentParser.privacy: Your input is processed locally in your browser and is not uploaded to our server.

faq.a: It is a text value sent by many browsers and clients to describe the software making a request.
It may reveal browser family, operating system, device class, rendering engine, and compatibility markers.

faq.q: Should I rely on User-Agent for feature detection?
faq.a: Use feature detection when possible. User-Agent parsing is better for diagnostics, analytics grouping, and log investigation.

scrollDistanceRatio.seoDescription: Calculate the minimum and maximum single-scroll distance ratio needed to expose target elements within a planned scroll count range.
scrollDistanceRatio.intro: Calculate a practical single-scroll distance range as pixels, ratios, and percentages of viewport height. This is useful when you need to tune automated scrolling, exposure checks, or page-depth simulations so target elements can become visible within a planned number of scroll actions.
howToUse: Enter the viewport height, total page height, target element height, required visible ratio, and the expected minimum and maximum number of scroll actions. Then enter target element top positions, one per line, with an optional exposure rate after a comma.

The tool computes each target threshold, finds the deepest threshold, and returns a minimum and maximum single-scroll distance. Percent values are based on viewport height so they can be reused in scroll automation or configuration values.
scrollDistanceRatio.example: With a 932px viewport, 3000px page, 280px target height, 50% visible ratio, 3 to 6 scroll actions, and target tops at 200, 1200, and 2700, the calculator returns the percentage range needed for one scroll step.
scrollDistanceRatio.privacy: Your input is processed locally in your browser and is not uploaded to our server.

faq.a: The percentage is the recommended single-scroll distance divided by viewport height. For example, 50% means scrolling by half of the current viewport height.

faq.q: Why do I need a scroll count range?
faq.a: The minimum distance uses the deepest target threshold divided by the maximum scroll count. The maximum distance uses the maximum scrollable distance divided by the minimum scroll count.
It is the scrollTop value at which the configured portion of a target element can be exposed in the viewport, clamped to the page's scrollable range.

slsSqlGenerator.seoTitle: Natural Language to Aliyun SLS SQL
slsSqlGenerator.seoDescription: Enter a natural-language log query, parse it into structured JSON, and generate SQL for the Aliyun Log Service console.
slsSqlGenerator.h1: Natural Language to Aliyun SLS SQL
slsSqlGenerator.intro: Enter a natural-language query request. The server parses it into structured query JSON, validates fields and types, escapes SQL values, and renders an Aliyun Log Service SQL template.
howToUse: Describe the query you want, such as filtering by su, grouping by type and trackType, or finding trackIds that contain event 6-1 but not 7-1, then generate SQL.
DeepSeek only parses natural language into ParsedQuery JSON. Field validation, SQL escaping, and template rendering stay in server-side deterministic code.
slsSqlGenerator.example: For example, enter “query logs where su is https://www.fpffz.top/?channel=news01, grouped by type and trackType” to generate SLS SQL grouped by cc and ordered by cnt descending.
slsSqlGenerator.privacy: Your input is sent to the server-configured DeepSeek API for parsing. The API key is never exposed in the browser.

faq.a: No. The model only returns ParsedQuery JSON, and final SQL is rendered by server-side templates.
The generator supports group counts, raw filtered logs, records with A but without B, records with A but without B plus selected fields, and grouping by fields inside data JSON.

faq.q: How is su matched by default?
faq.a: su uses LIKE contains matching by default, which is useful for URLs with paths or query parameters. The tool also exposes exact matching.

faq.q: Can I run the generated SQL without changes?
faq.a: Use it as a starting point. Before production queries, verify field types, indexes, and SLS dialect functions for your project.

json.outputPlaceholder: Formatted or minified JSON will appear here.
json.emptyError: Paste JSON input before running this action.

timestamp.timestampError: Enter a 10-digit seconds timestamp or a 13-digit milliseconds timestamp.
timestamp.dateRequired: Choose or enter a date and time.
timestamp.exampleLoaded: Example values loaded. Run either conversion action.

url.placeholder: Paste a URL, query value, or encoded string.
url.decodeError: The input is not a valid URL-encoded component.

base64.decodeError: The input is not valid Base64 text.

ua.emptyError: Paste a User-Agent string before parsing.

scrollDistanceRatio.targetsHelp: Enter one target per line. Format: top, optional exposureRate.
scrollDistanceRatio.positiveNumber: Viewport height, page height, and target height must be greater than 0.
scrollDistanceRatio.visibleRatioError: Visible ratio must be between 0 and 1.
scrollDistanceRatio.scrollCountError: Scroll counts must be greater than 0, and minimum count cannot be greater than maximum count.
scrollDistanceRatio.invalidTarget: Each target line must start with a numeric top value and may include a numeric exposure rate.

templates.missingB: Records with A and without B

sls.notice: Generated SQL is a template. Adjust field names and string or number types to match your own SLS log schema.
